Based on the retrieved chunks from the **PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. — Amended and Restated Strategic Licensing, Distribution and Marketing Agreement**, here is a breakdown of the "Agreement Date" provisions that warrant legal review:

---

## ⚠️ Agreement Date — Sections Flagged for Legal Review

> **Source:** PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. - A/R Strategic Licensing, Distribution and Marketing Agreement

---

### 🔴 1. Definition & Dual-Date Structure (Preamble)
> *"This Agreement is made on **October 15, 2009** (the 'Agreement Date') and is effective as of the **Effective Date** (as defined below)..."*

**Why review?** The Agreement Date and the Effective Date are **two distinct dates**. A lawyer should confirm what the "Effective Date" is, how it differs from October 15, 2009, and which date governs the commencement of obligations.

---

### 🔴 2. Liability Cutoff — NDA Maintenance (Section on Transferred NDA)
> *"PPI shall retain all liabilities with respect to the foregoing obligations to the extent relating to periods **prior to the Agreement Date**. Commencing as of the Agreement Date, EKR shall... be responsible for all liabilities..."*

**Why review?** The Agreement Date acts as a **hard liability cutoff**. A lawyer should verify this date is accurate and that all pre-Agreement Date obligations of PPI are clearly documented and accounted for.

---

### 🔴 3. Liability Cutoff — Required Studies (Post-Marketing/Phase IV)
> *"PPI shall be responsible for all costs and liabilities incurred **prior to the Agreement Date** with respect to the Required Studies, and shall indemnify and hold harmless EKR from such costs and liabilities."*

**Why review?** This ties **indemnification obligations** directly to the Agreement Date. Any ambiguity in the date could expose either party to unexpected financial liability for Required Studies.

---

### 🔴 4. FDA Reporting Responsibility — Adverse Events
> *"PPI shall be responsible... to report all charges, complaints or claims reportable to the FDA... to the extent such charges, complaints or claims are made **prior to the Agreement Date**. EKR shall be responsible... to the extent such charges, complaints or claims are made **after the Agreement Date**."*

**Why review?** FDA reporting obligations are split at the Agreement Date. A lawyer should ensure there are **no gaps or overlaps** in coverage, particularly for adverse events that may have been initiated before but reported after the Agreement Date.

---

### 🔴 5. Supply Agreement Applicability
> *"'Supply Agreement' Means: (i) with respect to periods **between the Effective Date and the Agreement Date**, that certain Supply Agreement entered into by the Parties on the Effective Date and (ii) with respect to periods **on or after the Agreement Date**, that certain Amended and Restated Supply Agreement..."*

**Why review?** Two different Supply Agreements govern different time periods, split at the Agreement Date. A lawyer should confirm the transition between the two is seamless and that no obligations fall into an unaddressed gap.

---

### 🔴 6. Delivery of Materials
> *"The Parties acknowledge that **prior to the Agreement Date**, PPI has delivered to EKR (i) all existing PPI produced Promotional Materials (if any) and (ii) any existing market research in its possession related to the Product."*

**Why review?** This is a **factual acknowledgment** tied to the Agreement Date. If any materials were not actually delivered by that date, this clause could create a dispute. A lawyer should verify completeness of delivery.

---

## 📋 Summary Table

| Section | Risk | Tied to Agreement Date |
|---|---|---|
| Preamble / Definition | Dual-date ambiguity | ✅ |
| NDA Maintenance Liability | Liability cutoff | ✅ |
| Required Studies Indemnification | Financial exposure | ✅ |
| FDA Adverse Event Reporting | Regulatory compliance gap | ✅ |
| Supply Agreement Transition | Contractual continuity | ✅ |
| Delivery of Materials | Factual accuracy / dispute risk | ✅ |

---

> **⚠️ Note:** The Agreement Date of **October 15, 2009** is used as a **pivotal cutoff point** throughout this contract for liabilities, responsibilities, and regulatory obligations. Any error or ambiguity in this date could have significant legal and financial consequences. Legal review of all the above sections is strongly recommended.

---

## ⚠️ Cap on Liability — Clauses Flagged for Legal Review

### 🔴 Clause Requiring Review: Limitation of Damages (Section 9.1)

> **"NEITHER PARTY WILL BE LIABLE FOR ANY SPECIAL, INDIRECT, CONSEQUENTIAL OR INCIDENTAL DAMAGES ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT, HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE), AND EVEN IF SUCH PARTY HAS BEEN ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF SUCH DAMAGES."**

**Why this needs legal review:**
- This is a **broad mutual exclusion** of special, indirect, consequential, and incidental damages. This could significantly limit a party's ability to recover meaningful damages in the event of a material breach.
- The clause applies **"however caused and on any theory of liability"** — including negligence — which is an exceptionally wide scope.
- There is **no carve-out** for gross negligence, willful misconduct, fraud, or indemnification obligations, which are commonly negotiated exceptions. A lawyer should assess whether such carve-outs are needed.
- There is **no maximum monetary cap** (dollar ceiling) specified in the retrieved text, which may be a gap worth addressing.

---

### 🟡 Related Clause to Review: Indemnification Obligations (Section 8)

The contract includes mutual indemnification provisions covering:
- Breach of representations, warranties, or covenants
- IP infringement claims
- Operation of respective sites/products

**Why this needs legal review:**
- The **Limitation of Damages clause (Section 9.1) does not explicitly state whether it overrides or coexists with the indemnification obligations** in Section 8. This ambiguity — i.e., whether consequential damages are excluded even in indemnification scenarios — is a common point of dispute and should be clarified by a lawyer.

---

### 🟡 Notable Absence: No Time Limitation for Bringing Claims

The retrieved contract text **does not include any time limitation** (i.e., a contractual statute of limitations) restricting when a party may bring a claim for breach. A lawyer should assess whether this gap is acceptable or whether a limitation period should be negotiated.

---

### 🟡 Notable Absence: No Maximum Recovery Cap (Dollar Amount)

The contract **does not specify a maximum dollar amount** for liability recovery. Combined with the exclusion of consequential damages, this means direct damages are theoretically uncapped. A lawyer should evaluate whether a monetary ceiling should be added.

---

## Summary Table

| Issue | Present in Contract | Flagged for Review |
|---|---|---|
| Exclusion of consequential/indirect damages | ✅ Yes (Section 9.1) | 🔴 Yes — very broad, no carve-outs |
| Maximum monetary recovery cap | ❌ Not found | 🟡 Yes — absence may be a gap |
| Time limitation for bringing claims | ❌ Not found | 🟡 Yes — absence may be a gap |
| Interaction of damages exclusion with indemnification | ⚠️ Ambiguous | 🔴 Yes — needs clarification |

> **Disclaimer:** This is an informational summary based on the contract text retrieved from the knowledge base. It does not constitute legal advice. A qualified attorney should review all flagged provisions before execution.

Based on the retrieved content from the **WEBHELPCOMINC_03_22_2000-EX-10.8-HOSTING AGREEMENT**, here are the relevant provisions related to **Non-Transferable License** that should be flagged for legal review:

---

## ⚠️ Provisions Requiring Legal Review: Non-Transferable License

### 1. 🔴 Explicit Non-Transferable License Grant
> *"eGain grants Customers a **non-exclusive, non-transferable, limited license**, with right to sublicense solely to Users, to use such Software only in connection with the Hosting Services."*

**Why flag this?**
- The license is expressly **non-transferable**, meaning the Customer cannot transfer the license to any third party.
- The only sublicensing right permitted is **solely to Users** (i.e., the Customer's own end users), and only in connection with the Hosting Services. Any broader sublicensing would be a breach.

---

### 2. 🔴 Broad Restriction on Assignment of Rights/Licenses
> *"Customer agrees that, except as expressly set forth in this Section and in Section 11, it will **not rent, lease, sublicense, re-sell, time-share or otherwise assign to any third party** this Agreement or any of Customer's rights or licenses to access the Software or the eGain System, nor shall Customer use, or authorize others to use, the Software, Hosting Services or the eGain System to operate a service bureau."*

**Why flag this?**
- This is a **broad prohibition** covering a wide range of transfer-like activities (renting, leasing, sublicensing, reselling, time-sharing, service bureau use).
- The carve-out references "Section 11," which should be reviewed to confirm the full scope of permitted exceptions.

---

### 3. 🟡 General Assignment Restriction (Applies to Both Parties)
> *"**Neither party may assign or otherwise transfer its rights and/or obligations** under this Agreement without the prior written consent of the other party. Notwithstanding the foregoing, no consent shall be required for an assignment of this Agreement made pursuant to a **merger, consolidation, or the acquisition of all or substantially all of the business and assets** of a party."*

**Why flag this?**
- Assignment of the entire agreement (including the license) requires **prior written consent** from the other party.
- There is a **M&A carve-out** — consent is not needed in cases of merger, consolidation, or asset acquisition. A lawyer should assess whether this carve-out adequately protects the Customer's interests in a future transaction.

---

## Summary Table

| Provision | Risk Level | Key Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Non-transferable license grant | 🔴 High | License explicitly cannot be transferred to third parties |
| Prohibition on sublicense/resale/assignment | 🔴 High | Broad list of restricted transfer activities; service bureau use prohibited |
| General assignment restriction with M&A carve-out | 🟡 Medium | Consent required for assignment; M&A exception scope should be verified |

---

**Recommendation:** A lawyer should review these clauses to assess (1) whether the non-transferability restrictions are acceptable given the Customer's business needs, (2) the adequacy of the M&A carve-out for future corporate transactions, and (3) whether the permitted sublicense "solely to Users" is sufficiently broad for the Customer's intended use case.

- Excite will **not** propose, solicit, or negotiate offers from entities other than Sponsor for retail music store sponsorships until **[****]** prior to expiration.

> ⚠️ **Note:** Key time periods have been **redacted** (`[****]`) in the contract. A lawyer should confirm the actual durations, as they directly govern the enforceability and timing of this right.

---

### 🔴 2. Right of First Offer — Third-Party Sponsorship Deals (Section 1(d)(iii)–(iv))

> *"In the event that Excite intends to enter into an agreement with a third party with respect to retail music store sponsorships of the Excite Site before the expiration of the term of the Agreement, Excite will deliver to Sponsor a written notice describing the relevant opportunity."*

Key mechanics:
- Sponsor has **[****]** after receipt of written notice to respond and indicate it is prepared to enter into an agreement **on the same terms and conditions** as Excite proposes to accept from the third party.
- If Sponsor **rejects or fails to respond** within that period, Excite may proceed with the third party — provided the terms are **not less favorable to Excite** than those previously offered by Sponsor — within the subsequent **ninety (90) days**.

> ⚠️ **Note:** The response window for Sponsor is redacted (`[****]`). A lawyer should verify this period, as it defines how long Sponsor has to exercise this right.

### 🔴 3. Quasi-ROFR — Music Products Availability (Section 1(a))

> *"Excite may make available opportunities on the Excite Site to purchase Music Products from parties other than Sponsor if such Music Products are not available from Sponsor so long as, prior to entering into arrangements... Excite notifies Sponsor of its interest in the Music Products and gives Sponsor **thirty (30) days** to make the desired Music Products available through the Sponsor Site."*

> Additionally: *"In no event will Excite enter into arrangements to make available opportunities to purchase Music Products from parties other than Sponsor that would prevent Sponsor from being the exclusive source of such Music Products on the Excite Site once the Music Products become available through Sponsor."*

## ⚠️ Non-Compete Provisions — Legal Review Recommended

**Source:** *TUNIUCORP_03_06_2014-EX-10-COOPERATION AGREEMENT*

Yes, the contract contains significant non-compete and exclusivity restrictions that warrant careful legal review. The relevant provisions are found in **Article 2 (Exclusive Cooperation)** and are highlighted below.

---

### 🔴 Clause 1 — Exclusivity Restriction on Party A
> *"Party A irrevocably undertakes that Party A will take Party B as its **exclusive and sole partner** to provide the business consultancy and technical services as well as technical consultancy to Party A and its subsidiaries. **Party A shall not establish any same or similar cooperative relationship with any third party** in respect of such businesses nor shall it make any same or similar arrangement, **unless with the prior written consent of Party B.**"*

**⚠️ Why this needs review:** Party A is locked into an exclusive arrangement with Party B and cannot engage any third party for similar services without Party B's written consent. This is a broad restriction on Party A's operational freedom.

---

### 🔴 Clause 3 — Broad Non-Compete Obligation on Party A
> *"Party A irrevocably undertakes that, **without Party B's consent, Party A shall not conduct any other business or make any commercial arrangement**, including without limitation being engaged in or otherwise participating in **any commercial activities and businesses independently or together with any other person or entity**, nor shall it carry out **any activities that may be competitive with or cause adverse effect to Party B's business.**"*

**⚠️ Why this needs review:** This is an extremely broad restriction. It prohibits Party A from:
- Conducting **any** other business independently or with others
- Engaging in **any** activity that could be "competitive with or cause adverse effect" to Party B — a vague and expansive standard
- There is **no defined geographic scope, time limit beyond the Term, or industry carve-out** specified in the retrieved text

---

### 🔴 Clause 4 — Waiver of Right to Challenge
> *"Party A hereby expressly undertakes that it **waives the right to request amendment and revocation of any term of this Agreement** on the ground of material misunderstanding or unconscionability... or is raised against the provisions under which **Party A is prohibited from having any cooperation with a third party and conducting any businesses other than those agreed hereunder.**"*

**⚠️ Why this needs review:** Party A has pre-emptively waived its right to challenge the non-compete and exclusivity terms even on grounds of unconscionability or material misunderstanding. This significantly limits Party A's legal recourse.

---

### 📋 Summary of Key Concerns for Legal Counsel

| Issue | Risk Level |
|---|---|
| Exclusive partner obligation (no third-party engagement) | 🔴 High |
| Broad prohibition on all other business activities | 🔴 High |
| Vague standard ("adverse effect to Party B's business") | 🔴 High |
| No defined geographic or sector scope | 🟡 Medium |
| Waiver of right to challenge on unconscionability grounds | 🔴 High |
| Governed by PRC law — enforceability under local law | 🟡 Medium |

> **Recommendation:** These clauses are heavily one-sided in favor of **Party B**. A lawyer — particularly one familiar with **PRC contract law** — should review whether these restrictions are enforceable, proportionate, and consistent with applicable regulations before signing.

Based on the retrieved content from the **STWRESOURCESHOLDINGCORP_08_06_2014-EX-10.1-COOPERATION AGREEMENT**, this contract contains **directly conflicting Third Party Beneficiary provisions** that warrant careful legal review. Here is the analysis:

---

## ⚠️ Third Party Beneficiary — Clauses Requiring Legal Review

### 🔴 Clause 1: Section 17 — Authorized Third Party Beneficiary *(High Risk — Needs Legal Review)*

> *"STW shall be listed as a third party beneficiary to any contracts to guarantee payment of any of the obligations of this contract between COFS and Pecos County or any other entity or individual."*

**Why this needs review:**
- This clause **explicitly grants STW third-party beneficiary status** in future contracts between COFS and Pecos County (or any other entity/individual).
- This means STW — a contracting party here — gains the right to **enforce payment obligations** in separate, future contracts to which it is not a direct signatory.
- The scope is notably broad: it covers contracts with **"any other entity or individual,"** which could have wide-reaching implications.

---

### 🔴 Clause 2: Section 29 — No Partnership, Agency, or Third Party Beneficiaries Intended *(High Risk — Direct Conflict with Section 17)*

> *"Nothing in this AGREEMENT shall be construed to confer any right, privilege or benefit on, or to otherwise create any vested right or third-party beneficiary relationship with any person or entity not a party to the AGREEMENT, **unless otherwise provided in this AGREEMENT**."*

**Why this needs review:**
- This clause **generally excludes** third-party beneficiary rights — but contains a critical carve-out: *"unless otherwise provided in this AGREEMENT."*
- That carve-out directly references (and preserves) Section 17 above, creating a **tension between the general exclusion and the specific grant**.
- A lawyer should assess whether this carve-out is sufficient to protect the intended scope of Section 17, or whether it creates ambiguity about what other third-party rights may or may not exist.

---

## 📋 Summary Table

| Section | Clause Type | Key Issue | Review Needed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Section 17** | Authorized Third Party Beneficiary | STW granted beneficiary rights in future COFS contracts with third parties | ✅ Yes |
| **Section 29** | No Third Party Beneficiaries (with carve-out) | General exclusion conflicts with Section 17; carve-out language may be ambiguous | ✅ Yes |

---

## Key Legal Questions for Counsel

1. **Conflict between Sections 17 and 29**: Does the "unless otherwise provided" carve-out in Section 29 fully and clearly preserve STW's rights under Section 17, or does it create interpretive risk?
2. **Scope of Section 17**: The phrase *"any other entity or individual"* is very broad — does this expose COFS to unintended obligations in all future contracts?

> *"Neither party shall, without the **prior written consent** (not to be unreasonably withheld or delayed) of the other party having been obtained, assign or transfer this Agreement to any person or entity, in whole or in part (and any attempt to do so shall be void)..."*

**Yes — prior written consent is required**, with the following structure:

| Scenario | Consent Required? | Notes |
|---|---|---|

| Assignment to a **successor by merger** | ❌ No consent needed | Permitted without consent |
| Sale/transfer of **all or substantially all assets/business** | ❌ No consent needed | Permitted, but acquirer must confirm in writing agreement to be bound by all terms; assignor remains liable |

## 🔴 Key Provisions Requiring Lawyer Review

### 1. **Columbia's Unilateral Right to Withhold Consent (Fleet Assignments)**
> *"Notwithstanding the foregoing, it shall not be deemed unreasonable for Columbia to withhold consent, to any proposed or attempted assignment (including by merger or sale) by Fleet to a party which is not an Affiliate, if Columbia is not reasonably satisfied that the assignee possesses the **management, finances, personnel, capabilities and facilities** to perform fully the obligations of Fleet hereunder."*

⚠️ **Why this needs review:** This carve-out gives Columbia a broad, subjective basis to block Fleet's assignments — even in M&A scenarios. The criteria (management, finances, personnel, capabilities, facilities) are not precisely defined, which could give Columbia significant leverage to block a deal. ### 3. **Void Assignment Clause**

⚠️ **Why this needs review:** Any unauthorized assignment is rendered completely void — not merely voidable. This is a strict consequence and could have significant implications in corporate restructuring or M&A contexts. Legal counsel should confirm this aligns with applicable law (the agreement is governed by **English law**).

## ⚠️ IP Ownership Assignment — Clauses Requiring Legal Review

**Source:** *Dova Pharmaceuticals Inc. — Co-Promotion Agreement (EX-10.2, 10-Q, 11/8/2018)*

The contract **does contain significant IP ownership assignment provisions** that warrant careful legal review. Here are the specific clauses flagged:

---

### 🔴 1. Broad Assignment of Valeant-Created IP to Dova
> *"Dova shall have and retain sole and exclusive right, title and interest in and to all inventions, developments, discoveries, writings, trade secrets, Know-How, methods, practices, procedures, designs, improvements and other technology... made, discovered, conceived, reduced to practice or generated by **Valeant** (or its employees or representatives) in performing its activities pursuant to this Agreement to the extent primarily related to the Product or which is otherwise derived from the Confidential Information of Dova ('Inventions'). **Valeant agrees to assign, and hereby does assign**, to Dova... any and all right, title and interest that Valeant (or any such Affiliates, employees or other representatives) may have in or to any Invention."*

**Why this needs review (if you are Valeant):** Any IP created by Valeant or its employees during the performance of this agreement — if primarily related to the Product or derived from Dova's Confidential Information — is **automatically assigned to Dova**. This is a present-tense, self-executing assignment ("hereby does assign"), meaning no further action is needed for the transfer to occur.

---

### 🔴 2. Assignment of Trademarks and Copyrights
> *"The ownership, and all goodwill from the use, of any Dova Trademarks and Copyrights shall at all times vest in and inure to the benefit of Dova, and **Valeant shall assign, and hereby does assign**, any rights it may have in the foregoing to Dova."*

**Why this needs review:** Valeant irrevocably assigns any trademark or copyright rights it may develop or acquire back to Dova, including all goodwill generated through Valeant's own use of those marks.

---

### 🔴 3. Goodwill Inures to Dova
> *"All goodwill and improved reputation generated by Valeant's use of the Dova Trademarks and Copyrights shall inure to the benefit of Dova, and any use of the Dova Trademarks and Copyrights by Valeant shall cease at the end of the Term."*

**Why this needs review:** Even reputational value built by Valeant's promotional efforts flows entirely to Dova, and Valeant's rights to use those marks terminate at the end of the agreement.

---

### 🟡 4. No Ownership Rights from Know-How Disclosure
> *"To the extent that either Party hereto has disclosed, or in the future discloses, to the other Party any Know-How or other intellectual property... the other Party shall not acquire any ownership rights in such Know-How or other intellectual property by virtue of this Agreement or otherwise, and as between the Parties, all ownership rights therein shall remain with the disclosing Party."*

**Why this needs review:** While this clause protects both parties' pre-existing Know-How, it should be reviewed in conjunction with the Inventions clause above — there may be tension between what qualifies as "disclosed Know-How" (retained by discloser) vs. what qualifies as an "Invention derived from Dova's Confidential Information" (assigned to Dova).

---

### 🟡 5. No IP Rights Acquired by Valeant
> *"Valeant shall not acquire any license or other intellectual property interest, by implication or otherwise, in any technology, Know-How or other intellectual property owned or controlled by Dova or any of its Affiliates."*

**Why this needs review:** This is a blanket exclusion of any implied IP rights for Valeant, which could limit Valeant's ability to leverage innovations it contributes to the collaboration.

---

## Summary

| Clause | Direction of Assignment | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Inventions (§8.1) | Valeant → Dova | IP created during Term related to Product or derived from Dova's Confidential Information |
| Trademarks & Copyrights (§8.2) | Valeant → Dova | Any rights Valeant may acquire in Dova marks |
| Goodwill | Valeant → Dova | Generated through Valeant's use of Dova marks |
| Know-How Disclosure (§8.4) | Retained by disclosing party | Upon disclosure |

> **⚠️ Legal Recommendation:** If you are representing **Valeant**, these provisions are heavily one-sided. A lawyer should assess (1) the scope of "primarily related to the Product," (2) whether the self-executing assignment language is enforceable across all relevant jurisdictions, and (3) whether Valeant retains any rights to IP it creates that is *not* primarily related to the Product.

## ⚠️ Uncapped Liability — Clauses Requiring Legal Review

**Contract:** N2KINC_10_16_1997-EX-10.16 – Sponsorship Agreement (Excite & N2K Inc./Sponsor)

The contract **does contain provisions that create or imply uncapped liability** for both parties. The following clauses should be flagged for legal review:

---

### 🔴 1. Indemnification Carve-Outs from the Limitation of Liability Cap
**Section 14 – Limitation of Liability** (read together with **Section 13(c) and (d)**)

> *"EXCEPT UNDER SECTION 13(c) and (d), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY BE LIABLE TO THE OTHER FOR ANY SPECIAL, INCIDENTAL OR CONSEQUENTIAL DAMAGES..."*

> *"THE LIABILITY OF EXCITE FOR DAMAGES OR ALLEGED DAMAGES HEREUNDER, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, TORT OR ANY OTHER LEGAL THEORY, IS LIMITED TO, AND WILL NOT EXCEED, THE AMOUNTS ACTUALLY PAID BY SPONSOR TO EXCITE HEREUNDER."*

**⚠️ Why this is a concern:**
- The consequential damages exclusion is **explicitly carved out** for Sections 13(c) and (d) — the indemnification obligations. This means that for indemnified claims (including IP infringement and warranty breaches), **consequential and special damages may not be excluded**, potentially resulting in uncapped exposure.
- The direct damages cap (amounts paid by Sponsor to Excite) **only applies to Excite's liability**, and it is **not expressly stated to apply to Sponsor's liability**, leaving Sponsor's liability potentially uncapped even for direct damages.

---

### 🔴 2. IP Infringement Indemnity — Uncapped Exposure for Sponsor
**Section 13(d)**

> *"Sponsor will indemnify, defend and hold harmless Excite... from any and all third party claims, liability, damages and/or costs (including, but not limited to, attorneys fees) arising from:*
> *(i) The breach of any warranty, representation or covenant in this Agreement; or*
> *(ii) Any claim that the Content infringes or violates any third party's copyright, patent, trade secret, trademark, right of publicity or right of privacy or contains any defamatory content."*

**⚠️ Why this is a concern:**
- Sponsor's indemnification obligation for **IP infringement** (copyright, patent, trade secret, trademark, right of publicity/privacy, defamation) is **broad and contains no monetary cap**.
- Since Section 14's carve-out exempts Section 13(d) from the consequential damages exclusion, Sponsor could face **unlimited liability** including special and consequential damages for third-party IP claims.

---

### 🔴 3. Confidentiality & Trademark Breaches — Equitable Relief Without Limitation
**Section 15(a) – Dispute Resolution**

> *"The parties agree that any breach of either of the parties' obligations regarding trademarks, service marks or trade names and/or confidentiality would result in irreparable injury for which there is no adequate remedy at law. Therefore, in the event of any breach or threatened breach... the aggrieved party will be entitled to seek equitable relief in addition to its other available legal remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction."*

**⚠️ Why this is a concern:**
- Breaches of **confidentiality** and **trademark/service mark obligations** are explicitly carved out from the arbitration/mediation process and allow either party to seek **equitable relief (e.g., injunctions) in addition to all other legal remedies** — without any stated cap or limitation.
- The phrase "in addition to its other available legal remedies" means monetary damages remain available on top of equitable relief, with **no ceiling specified** for these breach types.

---

### 📋 Summary Table

| Clause | Section | Breach Type | Uncapped? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Indemnification carve-out from consequential damages exclusion | §14 + §13(c)(d) | All indemnified claims | ✅ Yes |
| Sponsor's IP infringement indemnity | §13(d) | Copyright, patent, trade secret, trademark, defamation | ✅ Yes |
| Excite's warranty/covenant breach indemnity | §13(c) | Warranty/representation/covenant breach | ✅ Yes |
| Equitable relief for confidentiality & trademark breaches | §15(a) | Confidentiality, trademarks/service marks | ✅ Yes (no cap stated) |
| Excite's general liability cap | §14 | All other claims | Capped (amounts paid by Sponsor) |

---

> **Recommendation:** A lawyer should review (1) whether the indemnification carve-outs in §14 were intentional and appropriate, (2) whether Sponsor's IP indemnity should be subject to a monetary cap or narrowed in scope, and (3) whether the equitable relief provision in §15(a) effectively bypasses the liability cap for confidentiality and trademark breaches.

**Source:** *ArconicRolledProductsCorp_20191217_10-12B_EX-2.7_11923804_EX-2.7_Trademark License Agreement*

---

### 📌 Relevant Provision Found — Section 10 (Assignment)

The contract **does** contain a Change of Control provision that warrants legal review. The key language reads:

> *"This Agreement may not be assigned by Licensee without the consent of Licensor which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld. Notwithstanding the foregoing, **no such consent of Licensor is required** under this Agreement in the event of a **Change of Control of Licensee** so long as:*
> *(a) the resulting, surviving or transferee Person assumes all the obligations of the Licensee by operation of Law or pursuant to an agreement in form and substance reasonably satisfactory to the Licensor; and*

| Issue | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Consent Requirement** | Generally required for assignment — but **waived** in a Change of Control scenario, subject to conditions |

| **No Termination Right** | Neither party is expressly granted a right to terminate solely due to a Change of Control |

1. **Licensor's discretion over assumption agreements** — Condition (a) allows Licensor to evaluate whether the assumption agreement is "reasonably satisfactory," which could be used to create friction or delay in a transaction.
2. **Affiliate license restriction** — Condition (b) could significantly limit the operational value of the license post-acquisition if the acquirer has a large affiliate network.
3. **No Licensor Change of Control protection** — If the Licensor undergoes a merger or asset sale, the Licensee has no express right to consent, receive notice, or terminate. This is a notable gap.
4. **No explicit definition of "Change of Control"** — The retrieved text does not include a definition of "Change of Control," which could create ambiguity about what transactions trigger this provision.

> 💡 **Bottom line:** The contract contains a **partial** Change of Control framework that is **one-sided** (Licensee only) and includes conditions that give Licensor residual discretion. ## ⚠️ Post-Termination Obligations — Clauses Requiring Legal Review

The contract (between **eGain Communications Corporation** and **Eliance Corporation / Customer**) contains several post-termination obligations across multiple categories:

---

### 1. 🔴 Immediate Cessation of Services & Access
> *"Upon any termination of this Agreement, eGain shall immediately cease providing all Hosting Services, and Customer shall no longer have access to the Software or the eGain System."*

**Why review:** There is **no transition period or wind-down window** provided. Customer loses access immediately upon termination, with no grace period to migrate data or operations. This could be operationally disruptive and should be negotiated if continuity is important.

---

### 2. 🔴 Conditional Delivery of Final Reports
> *"Except in the event of termination for Customer's breach, eGain shall provide Customer with an electronic copy of the final Reports (covering the month just prior to termination of this Agreement)."*

**Why review:** eGain's obligation to deliver final Reports is **conditioned on the reason for termination** — if Customer is in breach, it receives **no final Reports**. The scope of data returned is also limited to one month's Reports, with no mention of broader data portability or export rights for Customer's own data (e.g., Online Messages).

---

### 3. 🔴 eGain's Right to Retain Customer Data
> *"eGain shall be entitled to retain a copy (whether electronic or otherwise) of the Online Messages and the Reports for its records and internal purposes and shall not disclose such Online Messages or Reports to any third party except as permitted under Section 4."*

**Why review:** eGain retains **indefinite rights to hold copies of Customer's Online Messages and Reports** post-termination. There is no stated time limit on this retention, no deletion obligation, and no audit right for Customer. This raises **data privacy and confidentiality concerns**.

---

### 4. 🔴 Post-Termination Payment Obligation
> *"Within fifteen (15) days of any termination of this Agreement, Customer shall pay to eGain all unpaid fees accrued prior to termination."*

**Why review:** Customer has a **15-day payment deadline** for all accrued unpaid fees upon any termination. Legal should confirm whether this obligation is symmetric (i.e., does eGain owe Customer any refunds for pre-paid but undelivered services?) — the contract does not appear to address this.

---

### 5. 🔴 Survival Clause (Broad & Partially Unclear)
> *"[Sections] 2 and 12 and Exhibit A (as to amounts accrued but unpaid) shall survive any expiration or termination of this Agreement."*

**Why review:** The survival clause references **Section 2** (Provision of Services) and **Section 12** (Miscellaneous), but the retrieved text appears to have a **formatting/numbering gap** — it is unclear which specific subsections survive. Legal should verify the complete and accurate list of surviving provisions in the original document, as survival clauses can have significant long-term implications.

---

### 6. 🟡 Return of Confidential Information
> *"Upon any termination of this Agreement, each party shall return to the other party all confidential information of the other party, and all copies thereof, in the possession, custody or control of the party unless otherwise expressly provided in this Agreement."*

**Why review:** This is a **mutual obligation** to return all confidential information. However, it conflicts with eGain's separate right (noted above) to **retain copies of Online Messages and Reports**. Legal should assess whether this creates an internal inconsistency or whether the carve-out ("unless otherwise expressly provided") resolves the conflict.

---

### 7. 🟡 No Transition Assistance or Last-Buy Provisions
**Why review:** The contract contains **no transition assistance, data migration support, last-buy rights, or wind-down services** of any kind. If Customer needs help migrating to a new platform after termination, there is no contractual basis to require eGain's cooperation. Legal may want to negotiate a **transition services addendum**.

---

Here is a breakdown of the **Anti-Assignment** provisions found in the contract (**PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. – Strategic Licensing, Distribution and Marketing Agreement**) that warrant legal review:

---

## ⚖️ Anti-Assignment Clauses — Legal Review Highlights

### 1. 🟢 General Assignment (Section 20.1) — *No Consent Required*
> *"Either Party may assign its rights hereunder to an Affiliate or to any successor by merger, consolidation, sale of stock or other equity interests or the sale of substantially all of the assets of such Party **without the consent of the other Party**."*

**Key Point:** In standard assignment scenarios (affiliate transfers, mergers, asset sales), **no consent is required** from the other party. This is a broad carve-out that significantly limits each party's control over who ultimately holds the contract rights.

> *"For the avoidance of doubt, either Party may grant a security interest with respect to its rights under this Agreement in connection with a secured financing or similar transaction."*

**Key Point:** Rights can also be pledged as collateral in financing deals — again, without consent.

---

### 2. 🔴 Change of Control + Competing Product (Section 20.4) — *Express Consent Required* ⚠️
> *"Should there be a Change of Control of either Party resulting in the control of such Party by a Third Party which **markets or sells a Competing Product** in any part of the Territory, then the rights under this Agreement **may not be assigned without the express consent of the other Party**, which consent shall not be unreasonably withheld."*

**Key Points for Legal Review:**
- **Consent IS required**, but only in this specific scenario: a Change of Control where the acquiring third party sells a **Competing Product**.
- The consent standard is **"not unreasonably withheld"** — this is a qualified, not absolute, veto right.
- The definition of **"Change of Control"** is broad and includes: asset sales, mergers, and even a **sale of more than 50% of outstanding voting stock** of PPI or its Affiliates by a holder directly to a non-affiliate.
- **Risk:** If a competitor acquires either party, the other party has a consent right — but cannot arbitrarily block the assignment. The scope of what qualifies as a "Competing Product" is critical and should be carefully reviewed.

---

### 3. 🟡 Sub-Distribution (Section 20.2) — *Notice Required (Not Consent)* ⚠️
> *"EKR may appoint sub-distributors under this Agreement provided that EKR: (a) **informs PPI of the identity of any Third Party sub-distributor** (other than Affiliate companies) **prior to the execution** of any sub-distribution agreement..."*

**Key Points for Legal Review:**
- EKR can appoint third-party sub-distributors, but must **notify PPI in advance** of the sub-distributor's identity.
- PPI must also **approve the NDA form** (acceptance not to be unreasonably withheld).
- EKR must provide PPI with a **copy of the executed sub-distribution agreement** as soon as practicable.
- Importantly, **EKR remains primarily liable** to PPI for all obligations and any acts/omissions of the sub-distributor (Section 20.3).

---

## 🔍 Summary Table

| Scenario | Consent Required? | Notice Required? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Assignment to Affiliate or successor (merger, asset sale, stock sale) | ❌ No | ❌ No | Broad freedom to assign |
| Security interest / collateral pledge | ❌ No | ❌ No | Explicitly permitted |
| Change of Control → acquiring party sells a **Competing Product** | ✅ **Yes** (express consent) | N/A | Consent cannot be unreasonably withheld |
| EKR appointing a **Third Party sub-distributor** | ❌ No (NDA form approval only) | ✅ **Yes** (prior notice to PPI) | EKR remains liable |

---

## ⚠️ Areas Flagged for Lawyer Review

1. **Scope of "no consent" in Section 20.1** — The broad freedom to assign without consent (including in mergers and full asset sales) could expose a party to an unwanted counterparty with no recourse.
2. **"Competing Product" definition** — The trigger for the consent requirement in Section 20.4 hinges entirely on this definition. If it is vague or narrow, the consent protection may be easily circumvented.
3. **"Not unreasonably withheld" standard** — Even where consent IS required (Section 20.4), the withholding party's ability to block is legally limited. A lawyer should assess whether this provides adequate protection.
4. **50% voting stock transfer threshold** — The expanded Change of Control definition (triggering at >50% stock transfer) applies specifically to PPI and its Affiliates, which creates an asymmetry worth reviewing.

> 📌 *Source: PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. – Strategic Licensing, Distribution and Marketing Agreement, Sections 20.1, 20.2, 20.3, 20.4, and the "Change of Control" definition.*

---

## ⚠️ Termination For Convenience — Legal Review Highlights

**Source:** `TUNIUCORP_03_06_2014-EX-10-COOPERATION AGREEMENT`

---

### Direct Answer

**No — a party generally cannot terminate this contract without cause simply by giving notice and waiting.** The contract contains highly restrictive termination provisions. However, there are several clauses that carry significant legal risk and **should be reviewed by a lawyer**. Here are the key ones:

---

### 🔴 Clauses Requiring Legal Review

#### 1. Asymmetric Termination Right (Article 6.1.1)
> *"Party B shall have the right to terminate this Agreement in advance without the prior written consent from Party A, by sending a written notice to Party A **but Party A may not terminate or rescind this Agreement**."*

**Why it needs review:**
- **Only Party B** has a unilateral right to terminate by written notice — essentially a one-sided "termination for convenience" right.
- **Party A has no equivalent right.** This is a significant imbalance. A lawyer should assess whether this is enforceable and what protections (if any) Party A retains.

---

#### 2. Broad Waiver of Statutory Termination Rights (Article 6.3)
> *"Each Party hereby irrevocably waives other rights to terminate this Agreement it may have under any applicable laws, except for the rights of the Parties agreed under this Article 6."*

**Why it needs review:**
- Both parties have **irrevocably waived** any termination rights granted by applicable law (Chinese law), beyond what Article 6 explicitly provides.
- The word **"irrevocably"** is particularly consequential — a lawyer should assess whether such a blanket waiver is enforceable under PRC law and what rights are being surrendered.

---

#### 3. Waiver of Breach-Based Termination (Article 8.2)
> *"The non-breaching Party shall waive the right to request termination of this Agreement it may have according to any applicable laws as a result of the violation acts committed by the breaching Party."*

**Why it needs review:**
- Even if the other party **materially breaches** the contract, the non-breaching party **cannot terminate** — they are limited to demanding liquidated damages and specific performance.
- This severely limits Party A's remedies in practice. A lawyer should evaluate whether this clause is enforceable and whether it creates undue risk.

---

#### 4. Waiver of Misunderstanding/Unconscionability Claims (Article 6.4)
> *"Party A hereby expressly undertakes that it waives the right to request amendment and revocation of any term of this Agreement on the ground of material misunderstanding or unconscionability..."*

**Why it needs review:**
- Party A waives the right to challenge terms even on grounds of **unconscionability** — a fundamental legal protection.
- A lawyer should assess whether this waiver is valid under PRC law and whether it could be challenged.

---

#### 5. Force Majeure — No Termination Right (Article 10 / 6.3)
> *"Neither Party shall exercise the right to termination this Agreement that it may have under any applicable laws on the ground of occurrence of force majeure event."*

**Why it needs review:**
- Even a prolonged force majeure event (except Party B failing to provide services for **more than three consecutive years**) does not give either party a termination right.
- The threshold of **three consecutive years** is unusually high and should be evaluated by a lawyer.

---

### Summary Table

| Clause | Issue | Risk Level |
|---|---|---|
| Art. 6.1.1 | Only Party B can terminate unilaterally | 🔴 High |
| Art. 6.3 | Irrevocable waiver of all statutory termination rights | 🔴 High |
| Art. 8.2 | No termination even upon material breach | 🔴 High |
| Art. 6.4 | Waiver of unconscionability/misunderstanding claims | 🟠 Medium-High |
| Art. 10 | No termination for force majeure (except 3-year threshold) | 🟠 Medium |

---

> **⚖️ Recommendation:** This contract contains unusually restrictive and one-sided termination provisions. **Legal counsel — particularly one familiar with PRC contract law — should review this agreement before signing**, especially given the irrevocable waivers and the complete absence of a termination-for-convenience right for Party A.

> *"The amounts set forth for the **[***]** in each Production Schedule shall constitute a **firm purchase order and shall be binding upon Columbia** (each a 'Purchase Order') unless otherwise agreed in writing by both parties."*

#### 2. 🟡 Non-Binding Forecast (Moderate Priority)

> *"The amounts set forth for the following **[***]** shall constitute Columbia's **non-binding, good faith estimate** of the Product requirements of Columbia for such periods."*

**Why this needs review:**

## ⚠️ Exclusivity — Lawyer Review Required

**Source:** *TUNIUCORP_03_06_2014-EX-10-COOPERATION AGREEMENT*

The contract contains **significant and broad exclusivity provisions** that warrant careful legal review. These are found primarily in **Article 2 (Exclusive Cooperation)**. Below are the specific clauses flagged, with commentary on why each raises concern.

---

### 🚩 Flagged Clause 1 — Exclusive & Sole Partner Commitment
> *"Party A irrevocably undertakes that Party A will take Party B as its **exclusive and sole partner** to provide the business consultancy and technical services as well as technical consultancy to Party A and its subsidiaries. Party A shall not establish any same or similar cooperative relationship with any third party in respect of such businesses nor shall it make any same or similar arrangement, **unless with the prior written consent of Party B**."*

**Why it needs review:**
- This is a full **exclusive dealing commitment** — Party A is locked into sourcing all relevant business consultancy and technical services solely from Party B.
- The restriction extends to **Party A's subsidiaries**, significantly broadening its scope.
- The only escape valve requires **Party B's prior written consent**, giving Party B unilateral gatekeeping power.

---

### 🚩 Flagged Clause 2 — Broad Prohibition on Any Other Business or Commercial Activity
> *"Party A irrevocably undertakes that, without Party B's consent, Party A shall not conduct any other business or make any commercial arrangement, including without limitation being engaged in or otherwise participating in any commercial activities and businesses independently or together with any other person or entity, nor shall it carry out any activities that may be competitive with or cause adverse effect to Party B's business."*

**Why it needs review:**
- This goes **far beyond a standard exclusivity clause** — it effectively prohibits Party A from engaging in **any** independent commercial activity or collaboration without Party B's consent.
- The phrase *"cause adverse effect to Party B's business"* is **vague and undefined**, potentially giving Party B an extremely wide basis to restrict Party A's operations.
- This could be construed as an **anti-competitive restraint of trade** under applicable law.

---

### 🚩 Flagged Clause 3 — Irrevocable Waiver of Right to Challenge Exclusivity Terms
> *"Party A hereby expressly undertakes that it waives the right to request amendment and revocation of any term of this Agreement on the ground of material misunderstanding or unconscionability... or is raised against the provisions under which Party A is **prohibited from having any cooperation with a third party** and conducting any businesses other than those agreed hereunder."*

**Why it needs review:**
- Party A has **pre-emptively waived its right** to challenge the exclusivity restrictions, even on grounds of unconscionability or material misunderstanding.
- This significantly limits Party A's legal recourse if the exclusivity terms prove commercially harmful.

---

### 🚩 Flagged Clause 4 — Term Tied to Party B's Operational Lifespan
> *"The term of cooperation under this Agreement shall commence from the execution date hereof and **end on the expiration date of the operation term of Party B**."*

**Why it needs review:**
- The exclusivity obligations run for the **entire operational life of Party B**, which is an indefinite and potentially very long period.
- There is **no fixed end date** for the exclusivity commitment, and Party A has **no unilateral right to terminate** the agreement (only Party B may terminate early).

---

### 📋 Summary Table

| Clause | Exclusivity Risk | Severity |
|---|---|---|
| Art. 2(1) — Exclusive & sole partner | Full exclusive dealing; no third-party services without consent | 🔴 High |
| Art. 2(3) — No other business/commercial activity | Sweeping prohibition on independent or collaborative activity | 🔴 High |
| Art. 6(4) — Waiver of right to challenge | Bars Party A from contesting exclusivity terms | 🔴 High |
| Art. 5 — Term tied to Party B's existence | Indefinite exclusivity with no fixed end date | 🟠 Medium-High |

---

> **⚖️ Disclaimer:** This is a preliminary contract review for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice. A qualified lawyer should review these provisions — particularly in the context of PRC competition law and any applicable anti-monopoly regulations — before the agreement is executed or relied upon.

---

### ⚠️ Sections That Should Be Reviewed by a Lawyer

The following aspects of this clause raise significant legal concerns and warrant attorney review:

---

#### 1. 🔴 Redacted Duration (`[***]`)
The **time period** governing the non-solicitation restriction is entirely **redacted** (shown as `[***]`). This is critical because:
- It is **unknown whether the restriction applies during the contract, after termination, or both.**
- The duration directly determines the enforceability and commercial impact of the clause.
- **A lawyer must review the unredacted agreement** to assess whether the duration is reasonable and enforceable under applicable law.

---

#### 2. 🔴 Redacted Exceptions (`[***]`)
The clause notes that **Section 3.2 shall not apply to `[***]`** — meaning there are **carve-outs or exceptions** to the non-solicitation restriction that are also fully redacted. Without knowing these exceptions:
- The true scope of the restriction cannot be assessed.
- There may be important safe harbors (e.g., general public job postings, employees who independently apply) that are hidden.

#### 3. 🟡 Broad Scope of Covered Personnel
The restriction covers any personnel who had **"direct involvement"** with:
- The **Joint Steering Committee (JSC)**,
- **Valeant Activities** (including all Field Force Personnel), or
- **Dova's commercialization activities** for the Product.

This is a **broad category** that could encompass a large number of employees and contractors. A lawyer should assess whether this scope is proportionate and enforceable.

---

#### 4. 🟡 Mutual Obligation Binding Affiliates
The restriction applies to **both parties and all of their respective Affiliates**, which could significantly expand the universe of entities bound by this clause. Legal review should confirm whether affiliate obligations are appropriately defined and limited.

---

#### 5. 🟡 Consent Requirement — No Standard Specified
Solicitation is permitted only with **"the other Party's prior written consent"** — but the contract does **not specify** any standard for granting or withholding that consent (e.g., "not to be unreasonably withheld"). This gives each party broad discretion to block the other from hiring, which could be commercially problematic.

| Redacted exceptions (`[***]`) | 🔴 High | Yes — carve-outs unknown |

| Restriction extends to all Affiliates | 🟡 Medium | Yes — scope may be overbroad |
| No standard for withholding consent | 🟡 Medium | Yes — potential for abuse |

---

> **Bottom line:** A non-solicitation clause is present and applies to **both parties and their affiliates**. However, because the **duration and exceptions are redacted**, the full scope and enforceability of this restriction **cannot be determined from this document alone**. Legal counsel must review the unredacted agreement before any reliance is placed on this clause.

---

## ⚖️ Audit Rights — Legal Review Highlights

### ✅ Audit Rights Clause Found — **Legal Review Recommended**

The contract contains a substantive Audit Rights provision (replacing Section 10.1 of the Existing Agreement). Below are the key elements that warrant attorney review:

---

### 📋 Key Provisions

> *"Columbia QA, any other person appointed by Columbia, Columbia's customer, and/or any Regulatory Authority may conduct inspections and audits of Fleet's manufacturing facility, Columbia Equipment, quality control laboratories, and other quality systems relating to the manufacture and storage of the Product..."*

> *"Columbia or any other person appointed by Columbia shall have the right, in connection with any such audit, to inspect and obtain copies of any records or other documents and materials associated with or related to the manufacture of the Product."*

---

### 🚩 Areas Flagging for Legal Review

| Issue | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Broad Scope of Auditors** | The right extends not just to Columbia QA, but to **"any other person appointed by Columbia"** and **"Columbia's customer"** — a potentially broad and open-ended class of auditors with access to Fleet's facilities. |
| **Physical Location Access** | Auditors may access Fleet's **manufacturing facility, Columbia Equipment, quality control laboratories, and other quality systems** — this is broad physical access. |
| **Document & Records Access** | Columbia may **inspect and obtain copies** of any records or documents related to manufacture — consider whether this is appropriately scoped or overly broad. |
| **"For Cause" Audit Notice Period** | A "For Cause" audit requires only **3 business days' prior written notice** — this is a short window for Fleet to prepare. |
| **Standard Audit Notice** | Standard audits require only **"reasonable prior written notice"** — the term "reasonable" is undefined and could be disputed. |
| **Cost Allocation** | Audits by Columbia QA or persons appointed by Columbia are at **Columbia's sole cost and expense** — however, the cost burden on Fleet (staff time, disruption) is not addressed. |
| **No Frequency Limitation** | The clause does not appear to limit the **number or frequency** of audits, which could be operationally disruptive for Fleet. |
| **Confidentiality of Audit Findings** | There is no explicit provision in this clause governing how audit findings or copied records are to be treated confidentially by Columbia or its appointed auditors. |

---

### ⚠️ Summary Recommendation

**Yes — a lawyer should review this clause.** The audit rights are heavily weighted in Columbia's favor, granting broad access to Fleet's physical facilities, equipment, and records, with a wide class of potential auditors (including Columbia's own customers), short notice periods, and no apparent frequency cap. Fleet's counsel should assess whether appropriate guardrails (confidentiality obligations for auditors, frequency limits, clearer notice standards) should be negotiated.

**Source:** *ArconicRolledProductsCorp — Trademark License Agreement (EX-2.7, 12/17/2019)*

---

### Answer: Yes — The contract contains a license granted by one party to its counterparty.

The contract is a **Trademark License Agreement** in which the **Licensor** grants a license to the **Licensee** to use the trademark **"ARMX"** (the "Licensed Mark"). Below are the specific provisions that should be flagged for legal review.

---

## 🔴 Clauses to Review with a Lawyer

### 1. Grant of License *(Section 1.1)*
> *"Licensor on behalf of itself and its Affiliates hereby grants to Licensee the **limited licenses** to use and have used the Licensed Mark: (i) for the Licensed Products as set forth on Schedule 2; and (ii) as set forth on Schedule 1, concerning agreements entered into by Licensee prior to the Effective Date ('Existing Agreements')."*

⚠️ **Why review:** The grant is described as "limited," but the specific limitations are defined by reference to **Schedule 1 and Schedule 2**, which were not retrieved. A lawyer should confirm the full scope of what is and isn't permitted.

---

### 2. Sublicensing Rights *(Section 1.1, avoidance of doubt clause)*
> *"For avoidance of doubt, to the extent that any of the licenses granted by the terms of this Agreement include any right to sublicense, such right to sublicense shall extend to Licensee's subsidiaries and joint venturers."*

⚠️ **Why review:** The sublicense right is **conditional** ("to the extent that... any license includes any right to sublicense") — it is not an outright grant. A lawyer should clarify whether sublicensing is actually permitted and under what conditions.

---

### 3. Broad Scope of Licensed Purpose *(Recitals / Whereas Clauses)*
> *"Licensee wishes to obtain from Licensor... the right and license to **use, have used, manufacture, have manufactured, sell, have sold, advertise, have advertised, import, have imported, export, have exported, offer for sale, and have offered for sale** the Licensed Products using the Licensed Mark."*

⚠️ **Why review:** The intended scope is very broad. A lawyer should verify whether the operative grant in Section 1.1 actually delivers all of these rights, or whether the recitals overstate what is formally granted.

---

### 4. Goodwill & No Ownership Rights *(Section 1.2)*
> *"Licensee expressly recognizes and acknowledges that its use of the Licensed Mark shall inure solely to the benefit of Licensor, and shall not confer on Licensee any ownership rights to the Licensed Mark. Licensee agrees and covenants that it shall not challenge, contest, or take any actions inconsistent with Licensor's exclusive rights of ownership of the Licensed Mark."*

⚠️ **Why review:** This is a **non-challenge covenant** — a significant restriction on Licensee's rights. A lawyer should assess whether this is acceptable and enforceable in the relevant jurisdiction.

---

### 5. Quality Control & Licensor's Right to Terminate *(Section 1.5)*
> *"In the event that Licensee has not taken appropriate steps to restore such quality within **one-hundred twenty (120) days** after notification by Licensor, Licensor shall have the right to terminate this Agreement."*

⚠️ **Why review:** The Licensor retains broad quality control rights and a **unilateral termination right** tied to quality standards. A lawyer should assess whether the quality thresholds are sufficiently defined and whether the cure period is adequate.

---

### 6. No Warranty on the Licensed Mark *(Section 5.3)*
> *"Licensor, by this Agreement, makes **no warranties or guarantees, either express or implied**, arising by law or otherwise with regard to the Licensed Mark and/or the Licensed Products."*

⚠️ **Why review:** The Licensor provides **zero warranty** that the Licensed Mark is valid, enforceable, or non-infringing. This is a significant risk for the Licensee — a lawyer should advise on whether additional IP representations should be negotiated.

---
